sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgical options are limited to local control of small, symptomatic lesions in patients with limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome. Wide excision is not indicated as it does not contribute to control of KS [KS-D, MS-10].

Principles:
- Marginal excision is appropriate for small lesions; wide excision with goal of negative margins is not indicated [KS-D].
- Electrodesiccation and curettage can be used for local control of small symptomatic lesions [KS-D].
- Intralesional chemotherapy (vinblastine or bleomycin) is an option for small lesions, but pain from injection is common [KS-D].
- Cryotherapy should be performed by clinicians with expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy and is most effective for non-nodular, smaller lesions (<1 cm) [KS-D].

Resectability criteria: Not applicable in traditional sense; surgery is considered for local control of small lesions when systemic therapy is not feasible or for cosmetic purposes. Wide excision is not recommended [KS-D].

Pre-operative optimisation: Optimize immune function and address reversible issues (e.g., control infections, optimize ART for PWH). Refer to lymphedema specialist if present [KS-B].

cards.name: Marginal excision
cards.detail:
Approach: Surgical removal of lesion with minimal margins.
Indications: Small, symptomatic cutaneous lesions in limited disease.
Key complications: Limited data; potential for wound healing issues, especially with lymphedema.
Operative mortality: Not specified in source.
Principle: Marginal excision is appropriate for small lesions; wide excision with goal of negative margins is not indicated [KS-D].

cards.name: Electrodesiccation and curettage
cards.detail:
Approach: Destruction of lesion using electrical current and scraping.
Indications: Small, symptomatic cutaneous lesions.
Key complications: Limited data; similar to minor surgical procedures.
Operative mortality: Not specified in source.
Principle: Electrodesiccation and curettage can be used for local control of small symptomatic lesions [KS-D].

cards.name: Intralesional chemotherapy
cards.detail:
Approach: Direct injection of chemotherapy into lesions (vinblastine 0.2 mg/mL or bleomycin 1.5–3 units/mL).
Indications: Small cutaneous or oral lesions; caution for plantar and palmar surfaces.
Key complications: Common: pain from injection; may persist for days. Ulceration possible.
Operative mortality: Not specified in source.
Principle: Intralesional chemotherapy (vinblastine or bleomycin) is an option for small lesions, but pain from injection is common [KS-D].

cards.name: Cryotherapy
cards.detail: Principle: Cryotherapy should be performed by clinicians with expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy and is most effective for non-nodular, smaller lesions (<1 cm) [KS-D].
